title: Java OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
root_cause: JVM heap exhausted — usually a memory leak or undersized -Xmx
fix: Profile with jmap/MAT to find the leak. Short-term: increase -Xmx. Long-term: fix the allocation pattern.

title: Kubernetes OOMKilled
root_cause: Container exceeded its memory limit and was killed by the kernel OOM killer
fix: Increase the container memory limit, or reduce the application's memory footprint. Check for memory leaks.

title: PostgreSQL connection refused
pattern: could not connect to server: Connection refused
root_cause: PostgreSQL is not running, not listening on the expected port, or a firewall/security group is blocking the connection
fix: Verify the database is running (pg_isready). Check listen_addresses in postgresql.conf. Verify security groups allow traffic on port 5432.

title: TLS certificate expired
root_cause: The TLS certificate has passed its expiry date and clients are rejecting the connection
fix: Renew the certificate. Set up automated renewal (certbot, ACM). Add a certificate expiry monitor.

title: No space left on device
root_cause: The filesystem is full — often due to unrotated logs, large temp files, or a growing database
fix: Identify large files with du -sh /* | sort -rh. Clean up logs and temp files. Add log rotation and disk usage alerts.

title: DNS resolution failure
root_cause: DNS lookup failed — the hostname doesn't resolve. Could be a misconfigured service URL, DNS server outage, or network partition
fix: Verify the hostname is correct. Check /etc/resolv.conf and DNS server availability. Try nslookup/dig to isolate DNS vs network issues.

title: Connection timed out
root_cause: TCP connection could not be established within the timeout window — typically a firewall drop, wrong port, or the target host is down
fix: Check if the target is reachable (telnet/nc). Verify security groups and NACLs. Check if the service is listening on the expected port.

title: Connection reset by peer
root_cause: The remote end forcibly closed the TCP connection — often caused by a crashed service, load balancer timeout, or TLS mismatch
fix: Check the remote service's health and logs. Verify TLS versions match. Check load balancer idle timeout settings.

title: Python ModuleNotFoundError
root_cause: A Python dependency is missing from the runtime environment — either not installed, wrong virtualenv, or a packaging issue
fix: Verify the correct virtualenv is activated. Run pip install -r requirements.txt. Check that the package name matches (some differ between pip name and import name).

title: Docker image pull failure
pattern: Error response from daemon: pull access denied
root_cause: Docker cannot pull the image — either the image doesn't exist, the tag is wrong, or registry authentication failed
fix: Verify the image name and tag. Run docker login to authenticate. Check IAM/service account permissions for private registries.

title: MySQL too many connections
root_cause: MySQL has hit its max_connections limit — usually caused by connection leaks, missing connection pooling, or a traffic spike
fix: Check for connection leaks (connections opened but never closed). Implement connection pooling. As a short-term fix, increase max_connections.

title: Redis OOM - maxmemory reached
pattern: OOM command not allowed when used memory > 'maxmemory'
root_cause: Redis has hit its maxmemory limit and the eviction policy is rejecting new writes
fix: Review the eviction policy (maxmemory-policy). Increase maxmemory if appropriate. Check for key patterns that are growing unbounded.

title: npm EACCES permission denied
root_cause: npm is trying to write to a directory it doesn't have permission for — usually a global install without proper prefix setup
fix: Use nvm to manage Node versions (avoids global permission issues). Or configure npm prefix to a user-owned directory.

title: Kubernetes CrashLoopBackOff
root_cause: The container keeps crashing and Kubernetes is backing off restart attempts. Root cause is in the container itself — check its logs
fix: Run kubectl logs <pod> --previous to see the crash output. Common causes: missing env vars, failed health checks, unhandled exceptions at startup.

title: Segmentation fault (core dumped)
root_cause: The process accessed memory it doesn't own — typically a bug in native code, a corrupted binary, or incompatible shared libraries
fix: Check for recent deployments or library updates. Run the binary under gdb/valgrind. Verify shared library compatibility with ldd.

title: AWS Lambda timeout
root_cause: Lambda function exceeded its configured timeout — usually due to slow downstream calls, cold starts, or inefficient code
fix: Increase the timeout if the workload is legitimately slow. Add timeouts to downstream HTTP calls. Consider provisioned concurrency for cold-start-sensitive functions.

title: Nginx 502 Bad Gateway
root_cause: Nginx could not get a valid response from the upstream server — the backend is down, overloaded, or returning malformed responses
fix: Check upstream server health and logs. Verify proxy_pass target is correct. Check for upstream timeout settings in nginx.conf.

title: Long GC pause / Stop-the-world
root_cause: JVM garbage collection is taking too long, causing application latency spikes — often due to large heaps or too many long-lived objects
fix: Review GC logs (-Xlog:gc*). Consider switching GC algorithm (G1/ZGC). Reduce object allocation rate. Profile with async-profiler.

title: Permission denied on file access
root_cause: The process does not have the required file system permissions — often caused by running as the wrong user or incorrect file ownership
fix: Check file ownership (ls -la) and the user the process runs as. Fix with chown/chmod. In containers, check the USER directive in the Dockerfile.

title: SSL/TLS handshake failure
root_cause: TLS handshake failed — usually a certificate chain issue, expired cert, hostname mismatch, or missing CA bundle
fix: Verify the certificate chain with openssl s_client. Check that the CA bundle is up to date. Ensure the hostname matches the certificate's SAN/CN.
